patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 19-year-old anxious, vague speaker. Start with: “My speech is really hard to understand and I’ve always been kind of floppy and weak.” Volunteer that you tire fast with walking/chores, people say your face looks “a little different,” and your smartwatch shows your heart rate doesn’t change much with stress/exercise. Speak briefly, with uncertainty (“I don’t know,” “maybe”), and worry you’re being judged.

If asked directly, share: late sitting/walking/talking; trouble with buttons/handwriting/utensils; tripping and stairs; snoring/unrefreshed sleep; dizziness on standing; constipation; difficulty following fast or multi-step conversations; sensitivity to loud/busy places.

Do not mention unless specifically asked: feeding trouble as baby, ear infections/hearing issue, very flexible joints, cold hands/feet/low sweating, brief “zoning out,” low gym stamina, dental crowding, light sensitivity.

Resist discussing bedwetting, bullying, panic in public, and sexual difficulties unless the clinician is gentle and asks privately.

Express misconceptions: you suspect Lyme, depression, autism “is the whole answer,” or thyroid issues. If empathy is shown, you soften, give more detail, and admit cost/insurance/job worries about therapy and heart testing.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Phelan-McDermid syndrome (22q13 deletion syndrome; SHANK3-related disorder)
required_evidence: Neurodevelopmental disorder with dysmorphic facial features
Severely impaired or absent speech
Generalized hypotonia
Autonomic dysfunction suggested by decreased heart rate variability
Genetic confirmation recommended: chromosomal microarray showing 22q13 deletion and/or SHANK3 pathogenic variant

medications: Melatonin 1-3 mg PO nightly
Polyethylene glycol 3350 0.4-0.8 g/kg/day PO daily
non_pharmacological: Confirm diagnosis with chromosomal microarray and/or SHANK3 sequencing; offer genetic counseling and recurrence risk discussion
Early intensive therapies: speech-language therapy with augmentative/alternative communication (AAC), occupational therapy, physical therapy
Evaluate decreased heart rate variability/autonomic symptoms: obtain ECG; consider cardiology referral and ambulatory monitoring if symptomatic (syncope, dizziness, palpitations)
Screen and manage common comorbidities: seizures (neurology/EEG if events), sleep disturbance, constipation/feeding issues, gastroesophageal reflux, vision/hearing problems
Individualized education plan (IEP) and behavioral supports; assess for autism spectrum features and sensory needs
Safety planning for hypotonia and developmental delay (fall risk, aspiration risk if feeding concerns)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 4-6 weeks to review genetic testing progress, therapy initiation, sleep/constipation response, and any cardiology evaluation results; earlier if syncope, seizures, or clinical deterioration

must_include: Working diagnosis is a genetic neurodevelopmental syndrome consistent with Phelan-McDermid (22q13/SHANK3)
Diagnosis is confirmed with genetic testing (chromosomal microarray ± SHANK3 sequencing)

Emphasize variability in severity and focus on supportive, developmental care
No curative medication; management is multidisciplinary and symptom-directed

Plan to evaluate autonomic/cardiac symptoms with ECG and cardiology input if clinically indicated

Discuss prognosis as individualized; highlight gains with early intervention

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for neurodevelopmental syndrome: developmental milestones, speech/language level, hypotonia onset, feeding/swallowing, seizures, sleep, constipation, behavior/autism features, regression, autonomic symptoms (syncope/palpitations), pregnancy/birth history, family history, prior genetic testing, and current meds/supplements.

Order confirmatory genetic evaluation: chromosomal microarray as first-line; consider SHANK3 sequencing if microarray nondiagnostic but suspicion remains. Order baseline tests only if clinically indicated by history/exam (e.g., ECG for syncope/palpitations).
Review results to support a working diagnosis consistent with Phelan-McDermid syndrome (22q13 deletion/SHANK3-related) and to guide counseling and referrals.

State working diagnosis as genetic neurodevelopmental syndrome consistent with Phelan-McDermid; acknowledge differential if testing pending/negative; link symptoms (hypotonia, severe speech impairment, dysmorphic features, possible autonomic differences).

Explain diagnosis and confirmation testing; emphasize variability, supportive care focus, and multidisciplinary management (PT/OT/speech, AAC). Provide red flags and resources, and recommend genetic counseling.
Critical safety check before any prescribing (e.g., melatonin, constipation agents).
Non-critical but expected check for interactions with current meds/supplements before prescribing symptom-directed therapies.

If symptom treatment is indicated (e.g., sleep or constipation), prescribe with dosing guidance, monitoring plan, side effects, and stop/seek-care precautions; otherwise document no medication needed.

If autonomic/cardiac symptoms are present or suspected, order ECG and/or cardiology referral-related testing; ensure contraindication check occurs before ordering if any order carries patient-specific risk (e.g., sedation, contrast).
Arrange follow-up to review genetic results, therapy progress, symptom response, and to coordinate referrals (genetics, neurology if seizures, cardiology if indicated, developmental pediatrics).
Close encounter after confirming understanding, providing written plan/resources, and ensuring safety-net instructions.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 62-year-old cooperative woman who speaks clearly. Start by saying you keep getting sudden, electric-shock pains on the right cheek/jaw. Volunteer: each jolt lasts seconds, comes in bursts then quiet periods; brushing teeth or touching your face can trigger it; you’re scared to eat or talk. If asked about triggers, add: light breeze, washing face, and chewing on that side can set it off. If asked about between attacks, say it feels normal. If asked about other symptoms, deny fever/feeling sick, facial weakness, numbness, or trouble speaking. Withhold unless specifically asked: a small “spot” near the upper lip/cheek that triggers attacks, you avoid brushing that side well, and you sleep poorly from worry. Only reveal if pressed about impact: you’ve been skipping meals and losing a little weight. Mention misconceptions: watery eye makes you wonder about cluster headache; you thought it might be a bad tooth. If clinician shows empathy, acknowledge feeling relieved but still anxious. Raise cost concerns: ask if meds can be taken only on bad days and whether lab monitoring for carbamazepine can be skipped if you feel fine.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Trigeminal neuralgia
required_evidence: Recurrent, brief (seconds to ~2 minutes) unilateral facial pain in trigeminal nerve distribution
Severe, electric shock-like/stabbing quality
Triggered by innocuous stimuli (e.g., chewing, talking, brushing teeth, light touch) with trigger zones
Pain-free intervals between attacks
No prominent cranial autonomic symptoms (tearing, rhinorrhea) that would favor cluster headache
Normal neurologic exam (no sensory loss); if abnormal, consider secondary causes and imaging

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Baseline within reference range (monitor for leukopenia/agranulocytosis on carbamazepine)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Baseline within reference range (monitor for thrombocytopenia on carbamazepine)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Sodium: Normal at baseline; monitor for hyponatremia (SIADH risk with carbamazepine)

medications: Carbamazepine 100 mg PO twice daily, titrate by 100-200 mg/day every 3-7 days to effect (typical 400-1200 mg/day in divided doses; max 1200 mg/day)
non_pharmacological: Avoid known triggers when feasible (e.g., cold wind, chewing on affected side) while initiating therapy

Discuss need for further evaluation if atypical features (bilateral pain, sensory loss, persistent pain) or poor response; consider MRI brain with attention to trigeminal nerve to assess secondary causes/vascular compression
If medication-refractory or intolerant: discuss referral to neurology/neurosurgery for options (microvascular decompression, percutaneous procedures, stereotactic radiosurgery)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks to assess pain control and adverse effects; repeat CBC and sodium within 2-4 weeks after initiation/titration, then periodically

must_include: Trigeminal neuralgia causes brief, severe, electric shock-like facial pain due to irritation of the trigeminal nerve
Why cluster headache is less likely (cluster typically lasts 15-180 minutes with tearing/red eye/nasal congestion and restlessness)
Red flags that would prompt urgent reassessment (new numbness/weakness, persistent pain between attacks, vision changes, fever, severe headache, bilateral symptoms)
Carbamazepine is first-line and often rapidly effective; dose will be titrated to balance benefit and side effects
Common side effects (drowsiness, dizziness, nausea, unsteadiness)
Serious adverse effects requiring urgent care (rash/blistering, fever/sore throat, easy bruising/bleeding, jaundice, severe confusion)
Need for lab monitoring (CBC for blood counts; sodium for hyponatremia)
Confirm medication allergies (penicillin rash; codeine intolerance) and clarify no known carbamazepine allergy
Review current medication list/OTC/herbals due to interaction potential (carbamazepine is a strong enzyme inducer)
Pregnancy/contraception counseling if applicable: carbamazepine is teratogenic and can reduce effectiveness of hormonal contraception
Avoid alcohol and other sedatives initially; do not drive/operate machinery until effects are known
Keep a trigger/pain diary to guide titration and confirm response
Seek care promptly for signs of severe rash or infection symptoms while on carbamazepine

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 12-year-old cooperative 7th-grade girl. Speak clearly and directly. Start by saying: “I keep getting these skin infections and chest infections over and over, and it’s happening again.” Volunteer: a painful red swollen warm bump on your thigh that’s getting bigger; a 1-week cough with thick mucus; on-and-off fevers and feeling very tired; and that you’ve had many skin and chest infections since childhood.

If asked for details, add: bumps may not hurt at first, then drain pus; frequent sinus and ear infections; mouth sores/white patches; itchy dry rashes; lots of antibiotics and sometimes they don’t work; you were told your IgE was very high.

Do NOT mention unless specifically asked: loose stools for months, cuts getting infected easily, getting winded on stairs, and dental problems.

Only if asked about vaginal/genital symptoms, admit embarrassedly to itching and thick discharge off and on.

Express misconceptions: you think it might be bad allergies/asthma, maybe MRSA boils, and you’ve worried about TB.

Bring up cost worries and admit you sometimes stretch/skip preventive meds and try home remedies to avoid visits. If the clinician is empathetic, relax, thank them, and share more honestly.

confounders: Severe atopic dermatitis can mimic/obscure bacterial skin infection and elevates IgE independent of immunodeficiency

Low BMI and hypoalbuminemia suggest undernutrition, increasing infection susceptibility and complicating interpretation of immune function

correct_diagnosis.primary: Hyper-IgE recurrent infection syndrome 5, autosomal recessive (DOCK8 deficiency)
acceptable_alternatives: Severe atopic dermatitis with recurrent skin infection

required_evidence: Markedly elevated total IgE (typically >2000 IU/mL, often much higher)
Peripheral eosinophilia (elevated absolute eosinophil count)
History of recurrent sinopulmonary and/or skin infections (often staphylococcal) and severe eczema/atopy
Evidence of recurrent viral skin infections (e.g., HSV, HPV, molluscum) supports DOCK8 over STAT3
CBC may show leukocytosis during infections; inflammatory markers (CRP/ESR) elevated during active infection

expected_lab_results.Total IgE: Markedly elevated (commonly >2000 IU/mL; may be >10,000 IU/mL)
expected_lab_results.Absolute eosinophil count: Elevated (eosinophilia, often >500 cells/µL; may be >1500 cells/µL)
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Normal to elevated; may be elevated during acute infection
expected_lab_results.CBC - Neutrophils (absolute): Normal to elevated during bacterial infection; not typically neutropenic
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Usually normal; may be mildly low if chronic inflammation/infection
expected_lab_results.C-reactive protein (CRP): Normal when well; elevated during active infection/inflammation
expected_lab_results.Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR): Normal when well; elevated during active infection/inflammation

medications: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 5 mg/kg/day (TMP component) PO divided BID (infection prophylaxis)
Acyclovir 20 mg/kg/dose PO QID (max 800 mg/dose) if recurrent HSV or frequent viral skin infections; consider prophylaxis based on history
Cetirizine 10 mg PO daily (or weight-based pediatric dosing) for pruritus/allergic symptoms
Triamcinolone 0.1% ointment apply thin layer to affected areas BID for eczema flares (avoid face/groin; use lower potency there)
Mupirocin 2% ointment apply to localized impetiginized lesions TID for 5-7 days (if superficial bacterial skin infection suspected)
non_pharmacological: Urgent referral to Clinical Immunology/Allergy for definitive evaluation and longitudinal management; confirmatory genetic testing if not already established

Vaccination review: avoid live vaccines if significant immunodeficiency suspected/confirmed; ensure household contacts appropriately vaccinated
Infection prevention counseling: early presentation for fever/skin lesions; hygiene and wound care; avoid sick contacts when possible
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Immunology follow-up within 1-2 weeks; sooner (24-72 hours) if treating an active infection or if symptoms worsen

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a cooperative but vague 6-year-old. Speak in short, simple sentences; let your caregiver answer details. Start by saying you get scary episodes when you don’t eat: shaky, sweaty, weak, very tired, sometimes nauseated or throwing up, especially after a stomach bug or going without food.

If asked, add: you can feel confused/irritable, heart races, anxious, sometimes bad belly pain. Say sweet drinks/fruit juice sometimes makes you feel worse, but you’ve gotten better fast after IV sugar or sugary drinks. Mention past ER visits for low blood sugar and dehydration.

Do NOT volunteer: you avoid fasting, snack at night, get worse within hours when sick and not eating, family says you act “spacey,” you don’t remember well.

Only if directly asked about accidents/sleep: admit (embarrassed) you sometimes wet the bed after a really bad shaky/confused night.

Bring up worries: easy bruising (blood problem), recent camping with headaches/neck stiffness (tick illness), flexible joints (connective tissue). If clinician shows empathy, say “thank you” and seem calmer.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase deficiency
acceptable_alternatives: Inborn error of gluconeogenesis causing fasting intolerance with lactic acidosis (suspected fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase deficiency)
required_evidence: Hypoglycemia on point-of-care glucose during fasting/illness
High anion gap metabolic acidosis (low HCO3- with low venous pH and elevated anion gap)
Elevated serum lactate consistent with impaired gluconeogenesis
Low or only mildly elevated serum beta-hydroxybutyrate relative to degree of hypoglycemia (inappropriately low ketosis)

Clinical context consistent with fasting intolerance/trigger by illness or reduced intake and absence of features supporting listed differentials (no hyperviscosity/neuropathy for Waldenstrom; no encephalitic syndrome for tick-borne encephalitis; no vascular EDS phenotype such as arterial rupture or characteristic habitus)

expected_lab_results.Point-of-care glucose: Low (hypoglycemia), often <60 mg/dL (<3.3 mmol/L) during decompensation
expected_lab_results.Serum beta-hydroxybutyrate: Low to mildly elevated for degree of hypoglycemia (inappropriately low/normal ketosis)
expected_lab_results.Serum lactate: Elevated (lactic acidosis), often >4 mmol/L and can be markedly high
expected_lab_results.Venous blood gas pH: Acidemia (e.g., pH <7.35; may be <7.25 if severe)
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (HCO3-): Low (e.g., <18 mmol/L; can be <12 mmol/L if severe)
expected_lab_results.Sodium: May be normal or mildly low (dilutional/hypovolemic depending on intake); monitor for shifts during dextrose therapy
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Often normal to elevated initially with acidosis; may fall after insulin response to dextrose—requires monitoring

medications: Dextrose 10% in water (D10W) IV bolus 2.5 mL/kg once, then continuous infusion to maintain euglycemia (goal glucose 80-120 mg/dL)
0.9% sodium chloride IV 10-20 mL/kg bolus once if clinically dehydrated, then maintenance fluids adjusted to include dextrose
Sodium bicarbonate IV 1-2 mEq/kg once if severe acidemia (pH <7.1) or hemodynamic instability, with repeat dosing guided by VBG and HCO3-
non_pharmacological: Immediate avoidance of fasting; initiate frequent carbohydrate-rich feeds as soon as tolerated
Avoid fructose, sucrose, and sorbitol (including in medications/IV solutions and dietary sources)
Treat precipitating illness (supportive care) and ensure adequate hydration
Serial monitoring: bedside glucose q15-30 min until stable then q1-2h; repeat lactate/VBG/electrolytes to track anion gap closure and potassium shifts
Early consultation/coordination with metabolic genetics; provide/confirm emergency sick-day plan
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Metabolic genetics follow-up within 1 week after stabilization (or prior to discharge if inpatient), with same-day/24-48h reassessment if symptoms recur

correct_diagnosis.primary: Chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH)
acceptable_alternatives: Chronic thromboembolic disease (CTED) with exercise limitation
Pulmonary hypertension (Group 4) due to chronic pulmonary emboli
required_evidence: History or evidence of chronic thromboembolism with pulmonary hypertension (e.g., persistent dyspnea/exercise intolerance >3 months after PE or unexplained PH with thromboembolic risk factors)
Presence of lupus anticoagulant suggesting antiphospholipid antibody–associated thrombophilia
Objective evidence of chronic thromboembolic obstruction on imaging (V/Q scan mismatched segmental perfusion defects preferred; or CTPA/angiography showing webs, bands, chronic occlusions)
Hemodynamic confirmation of pulmonary hypertension by right heart catheterization (mPAP >20 mmHg and PVR >2 Wood units) after adequate anticoagulation when applicable

Exclusion of primary parenchymal/interstitial lung disease as sole cause (differentials considered: cryptogenic organizing pneumonia, acute interstitial pneumonia, rheumatoid arthritis–associated ILD)

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Often normal; may be mildly elevated if chronic hypoxemia (secondary erythrocytosis) or mildly low if chronic disease/anticoagulation-related bleeding
expected_lab_results.Hematocrit: Often normal; can be mildly elevated with chronic hypoxemia
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Usually normal; assess baseline before anticoagulation and monitor for heparin-induced thrombocytopenia if heparin is used
expected_lab_results.WBC: Typically normal (helps argue against acute infection/inflammatory pneumonia as primary driver)
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Usually normal; may be low in advanced right heart failure due to neurohormonal activation
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Usually normal; monitor closely if diuretics are started
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Often normal; may be mildly elevated if chronic CO2 retention coexists, otherwise non-specific
expected_lab_results.Creatinine: Baseline needed for anticoagulant choice/dosing and for contrast-based imaging risk assessment; expected to be normal unless comorbid CKD

Enoxaparin 1 mg/kg SC every 12 hours (bridge until INR therapeutic if initiating warfarin; adjust/avoid in significant renal impairment)
Riociguat 1 mg PO three times daily (titrate every 2 weeks as tolerated up to 2.5 mg PO three times daily) for inoperable CTEPH or persistent/recurrent PH after pulmonary endarterectomy
Furosemide 20-40 mg PO daily as needed for right-sided volume overload (titrate to euvolemia; monitor electrolytes/renal function)
non_pharmacological: Refer to a specialized CTEPH center for operability assessment and consideration of pulmonary endarterectomy (first-line definitive therapy when operable)
If not surgical candidate or residual disease: evaluate for balloon pulmonary angioplasty in experienced center
Supplemental oxygen if hypoxemic (target SpO2 per local protocol, commonly ≥90%)
Supervised pulmonary rehabilitation/exercise training as tolerated
Vaccinations per chronic cardiopulmonary disease recommendations (influenza, pneumococcal) and smoking cessation if applicable
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 2-4 weeks for anticoagulation monitoring and symptom review; earlier (within 7-14 days) if starting diuretics or riociguat titration; ongoing specialty follow-up at CTEPH center

must_include: CTEPH means old/chronic blood clots have scarred/blocked lung arteries causing high pressure in the lungs and strain on the right heart

Anticoagulation is foundational and typically lifelong in CTEPH, especially with lupus anticoagulant
Pulmonary endarterectomy can be curative in operable disease; alternatives include balloon pulmonary angioplasty and/or riociguat for inoperable or residual disease
Key medication risks: bleeding with anticoagulants; low blood pressure/dizziness with riociguat; electrolyte/renal changes with diuretics

Iodinated contrast allergy: prior immediate hives—requires premedication plan and consideration of alternative imaging when feasible

Maintain activity within tolerance; gradual conditioning/pulmonary rehab recommended

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key CTEPH history, functional status, VTE history, anticoagulation use/adherence, bleeding risk, pregnancy/estrogen exposure, and red-flag symptoms; confirm known lupus anticoagulant and prior imaging; assess comorbidities relevant to therapy (renal/hepatic disease, hypotension).
Critical safety check before any ordering/prescribing: confirm details of penicillin allergy (urticarial rash) and iodinated contrast immediate hives; document severity and reaction timing.

Order/arrange confirmatory and staging tests per CTEPH pathway: V/Q scan (screening), transthoracic echocardiogram (RV strain), and referral-level testing planning for right heart catheterization and specialized pulmonary vascular imaging (CTPA or pulmonary angiography) with contrast-allergy precautions.

State working diagnosis of CTEPH and differential exclusions; link lupus anticoagulant to thrombosis risk and long-term anticoagulation need; note that definitive hemodynamic confirmation requires right heart catheterization.

Initiate/continue foundational anticoagulation (typically lifelong in CTEPH, especially with lupus anticoagulant) and symptom-directed therapy if indicated (e.g., diuretics for volume overload). If selecting warfarin, ensure INR monitoring plan. Do not prescribe riociguat until operability/inoperability assessment and contraindication checks are completed.

Meet communication milestones: explain CTEPH diagnosis, confirm need for further testing (V/Q, RHC, specialized imaging), explain treatment options (endarterectomy, BPA, riociguat), medication risks and monitoring (bleeding, hypotension, renal/electrolytes, INR/diet consistency), address allergies/contrast plan, urgent return precautions, and lifestyle guidance (avoid estrogen, activity/pulmonary rehab, warfarin consistency, avoid NSAIDs).
Arrange timely referral to a CTEPH specialty center for operability evaluation and definitive management; schedule INR/lab monitoring and follow-up to review results and adjust therapy.
Close encounter after safety checks, plan, education, and follow-up are completed.

You are ZhangXX, a 24-year-old man. You are vague and a bit evasive; answer briefly unless asked directly. Start with: “I’ve been having more shaking and weird jerky movements, and my seizures are acting up again.” Volunteer only: arms/legs twist or writhe on their own, a couple seizures after being mostly stable, more clumsy/tripping, more tired.

If asked for details, add: worse with stress/excitement, better at rest; facial grimaces/tongue movements; trouble swallowing/cough with drinks; speech harder to understand; brief staring spells; more forgetful/harder to follow instructions; mild unplanned weight loss; night leg cramps.

Do NOT mention unless specifically asked: choking on saliva at night, constipation, dizziness on standing, restless sleep, pale skin/SOB on stairs, dark stools.

Resist admitting: urine accidents, drooling, low mood/irritability, skipping seizure meds; only reveal after repeated gentle, nonjudgmental questioning. Express misconceptions: “Maybe it’s anxiety,” “Could it be Parkinson’s?” “Energy drinks?” “Just med side effects.” If clinician is empathetic, soften and share cost worries (ration meds, avoid visits/labs, distrust generics).

medications: valproic acid 500 mg PO twice daily
levetiracetam 750 mg PO twice daily
trihexyphenidyl 2 mg PO twice daily
ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day
cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 1000 IU PO daily
comorbidities: oropharyngeal dysphagia with intermittent aspiration risk
confounders: valproic acid adverse effects can contribute to tremor, parkinsonism, and gait instability, complicating attribution of extrapyramidal symptoms
trihexyphenidyl anticholinergic effects (cognitive slowing, dry mouth, constipation) can mimic/worsen baseline neurocognitive impairment
iron-deficiency anemia with tachycardia and fatigue may be misattributed to neurodegenerative progression or antiseizure medication effects
intermittent aspiration and poor nutrition can cause nonspecific weakness and weight loss, confounding assessment of neurologic decline
sleep deprivation and missed doses due to caregiver transitions increase seizure frequency and can be mistaken for disease progression

correct_diagnosis.primary: Intellectual disability-epilepsy-extrapyramidal syndrome (suspected genetic neurodevelopmental disorder; e.g., WWOX-related epileptic encephalopathy or other DEE with parkinsonism/dystonia phenotype)
acceptable_alternatives: Developmental and epileptic encephalopathy with extrapyramidal features (unspecified genetic etiology)
Cerebral palsy with epilepsy and dystonia/parkinsonism (if non-progressive course and perinatal injury evidence)
Juvenile neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis (CLN3) or other NCL (if progressive cognitive decline/vision loss)
Wilson disease (if hepatic signs, Kayser-Fleischer rings, low ceruloplasmin, dystonia/parkinsonism)
Mitochondrial disorder (e.g., Leigh syndrome spectrum) with epilepsy and movement disorder
Glutaric acidemia type 1 (if macrocephaly, dystonia crises, characteristic imaging/organic acids)
Drug-induced extrapyramidal symptoms in a patient with epilepsy/ID (from dopamine-blocking agents)
required_evidence: History of lifelong or early-onset intellectual/developmental disability
Epilepsy history (seizure semiology, onset age, frequency; EEG abnormalities if available)
Extrapyramidal signs (dystonia, parkinsonism, choreoathetosis, rigidity, bradykinesia) not fully explained by acute medication effects
Progression pattern (static vs progressive) and family history/consanguinity to support genetic syndrome
Medication review to exclude antipsychotic/metoclopramide-related EPS as primary cause
No evidence of acute infection/metabolic derangement explaining symptoms (basic vitals/clinical stability)

Clonazepam 0.25 mg PO BID (or 0.5 mg PO qHS) for myoclonus/dystonia/anxiety-related exacerbations; use lowest effective dose and reassess sedation/falls
Trihexyphenidyl 1 mg PO BID (titrate by 1 mg per dose weekly to 2-5 mg PO TID as tolerated) for dystonia/parkinsonian rigidity

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a cooperative 28-year-old daycare assistant speaking clearly. Start with: “I’ve had really bad diarrhea since yesterday and I feel weak and dizzy.” Volunteer early: watery diarrhea 10+ times/day, crampy wave-like lower belly pain, thirst/dry mouth, low fever/chills, and that you can’t keep up with drinking because it “goes right through.” Mention lightheadedness when standing.

If asked, add: blood and mucus in stool, urgent need to go/tenesmus, nausea without real vomiting, very little dark urine, racing heart/near-fainting on standing, headache/body aches, sick contact/stomach bug at daycare, and street vendor/picnic food 2 days ago.

Withhold unless specifically asked or symptoms worsen in conversation: barely urinating, cracked lips/dry eyes, a few pounds weight loss, and occasional fogginess/confusion when standing.

Resist/avoid mentioning anal soreness/leakage unless directly asked.

Express misconceptions: you assumed food poisoning, tried anti-diarrhea meds without help; worried about appendicitis or “IBS flare.” If clinician shows empathy, acknowledge and stay engaged. Ask about cost of IV/labs/admission and whether oral rehydration at home can replace hospital care; worry about missing work/returning to daycare.

medications: Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 0.03 mg/0.15 mg 1 tablet daily
Ondansetron ODT 4 mg every 8 hours as needed for nausea
confounders: Recent travel with consumption of street food (broadens differential to cholera, ETEC, typhoid)
Reports taking over-the-counter loperamide before presentation (may worsen dysentery and mask severity)
Recent course of azithromycin for sinusitis 2 weeks ago (can alter stool culture yield and raises concern for C. difficile)
Active menses with lower abdominal cramping (may obscure assessment of abdominal pain severity)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Shigellosis complicated by hypovolemic shock (severe dehydration from acute inflammatory diarrhea)
acceptable_alternatives: Acute bacterial dysentery with hypovolemic shock
Severe infectious gastroenteritis with hypovolemic shock
Septic shock secondary to shigellosis (if persistent hypotension despite fluids)
required_evidence: History consistent with acute infectious diarrhea/dysentery (frequent diarrhea, often bloody/mucoid, abdominal cramps, fever/tenesmus) with rapid volume loss
Signs of hypovolemia/shock (hypotension, tachycardia, cool/clammy skin, delayed capillary refill, altered mentation, oliguria)
Laboratory evidence of dehydration/hemoconcentration and/or metabolic acidosis (elevated hematocrit/hemoglobin; low bicarbonate)
No alternative clear cause of shock (e.g., hemorrhage, cardiogenic shock) on initial assessment
Clinical improvement in perfusion and blood pressure with aggressive isotonic fluid resuscitation (supports hypovolemia)

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Elevated (leukocytosis) consistent with acute bacterial infection/stress response
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal to mildly elevated (reactive thrombocytosis possible)
expected_lab_results.BMP - Sodium: Often normal to mildly low (hypovolemic hyponatremia possible) or mildly high if free-water deficit predominates
expected_lab_results.BMP - Potassium: Often low due to GI losses (may be normal early)
expected_lab_results.BMP - Chloride: Often elevated relative to bicarbonate (non-anion gap metabolic acidosis pattern)
expected_lab_results.BMP - Bicarbonate (CO2): Low (metabolic acidosis from diarrhea and hypoperfusion/lactate contribution)

medications: 0.9% sodium chloride 30 mL/kg IV bolus now; reassess and repeat boluses as needed to restore perfusion
Ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO twice daily for 3 days
Ondansetron 4 mg IV/PO every 6-8 hours as needed for nausea/vomiting
non_pharmacological: Immediate ABCs, place on monitor, establish 2 large-bore IVs, frequent vitals and mental status checks
If ongoing shock after initial fluids, continue aggressive isotonic fluids; consider ICU-level care and evaluate for septic shock if hypotension persists
Oral rehydration solution as soon as able; continue age-appropriate nutrition once tolerated
Strict intake/output monitoring; consider urinary catheter if unable to track urine output in shock

Agent asks about exposure risks relevant to shigellosis (daycare/close contacts, travel, contaminated food/water, MSM sexual exposure, outbreaks) and recent antibiotics/hospitalization to consider differentials (e.g., C. difficile).

Agent orders appropriate labs for severe diarrheal illness with suspected dehydration/shock: CBC, CMP (electrolytes/renal), magnesium as appropriate, lactate/venous blood gas if shock suspected, and stool testing (culture or PCR panel ± Shiga toxin) and blood cultures if febrile/septic features.

Before prescribing any antibiotic, agent performs required safety checks: confirms allergy history (penicillin urticaria) and performs contraindication check; if contraindication check cannot be completed due to missing data (e.g., pregnancy status, QT risk, tendon disorder history), agent asks targeted questions first.

Agent selects an appropriate non-penicillin empiric antibiotic option for shigellosis when indicated (e.g., ciprofloxacin, azithromycin, or ceftriaxone depending on severity/local resistance) and explains expected duration and goals (shorten illness, reduce transmission).

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old anxious but clear-speaking male forklift operator. Open with: brutal one-sided headaches that hit out of nowhere, mostly at night. Early in the visit, volunteer: sudden stabbing/burning pain around the left eye/temple, left-sided runny/stuffy nostril, left eye tearing/redness, you pace/rock because it’s unbearable, attacks come in clusters for weeks then months normal, often wake you 1–2 hours after sleep, and your dad had “cluster headaches.” If asked, add: lasts 45 min–2 hrs, 1–3/day in bad stretches, alcohol triggers quickly during a stretch, no nausea and light/sound don’t bother you, left sweating and slight droopy eyelid, normal between attacks; chronic morning phlegmy cough unchanged.
Withhold unless specifically asked: 10–20 min pre-attack restlessness, smaller pupil, seasonal pattern, smoking more worsens frequency. Be resistant about: feeling desperate/suicidal thoughts (no plan) and increased energy drinks/nicotine—only admit if asked directly with empathy.
Express misconceptions: you thought sinus infection or trigeminal neuralgia due to electric-shock face pains seconds long; worry touch triggers. Ask for quick, affordable fixes; doubt oxygen/injectables are covered. If clinician shows empathy, you soften, answer more fully, and ask about costs/work notes.

medications: Carbamazepine 200 mg PO twice daily
Albuterol HFA 90 mcg/actuation 2 puffs inhaled every 4-6 hours as needed
Tiotropium 18 mcg inhaled once daily
Fluticasone nasal spray 50 mcg 2 sprays per nostril once daily
confounders: Rhinorrhea and nasal congestion during attacks can mimic acute rhinosinusitis/allergic rhinitis
Trigeminal neuralgia (overlapping facial pain syndromes; may lead to misclassification of headache type)
Active tobacco use suggested by elevated carboxyhemoglobin (smoking is associated with cluster headache and chronic bronchitis; complicates respiratory symptoms)
Chronic cough/wheeze and mild hypoxemia can limit use/tolerance of certain acute headache therapies and can be misattributed to anxiety/panic during severe pain
Family history of similar headaches (familial clustering) may bias toward diagnosis despite need to exclude secondary causes

correct_diagnosis.primary: Cluster headache (familial), episodic
acceptable_alternatives: Trigeminal autonomic cephalalgia consistent with cluster headache
required_evidence: Severe unilateral orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain with attacks lasting ~15–180 minutes
Attack frequency from 1 every other day up to 8/day during a cluster period
Ipsilateral autonomic features during attacks (e.g., rhinorrhea/nasal congestion, lacrimation, conjunctival injection, ptosis/miosis, facial sweating)
Marked restlessness or agitation during attacks (vs. preference for stillness in migraine)
Family history of cluster headache (familial pattern)
Differential considered: trigeminal neuralgia (typically seconds-long electric shock-like pain triggered by touch/chewing, usually without prominent autonomic symptoms)

expected_lab_results.ESR: Normal (helps exclude giant cell arteritis in appropriate age group)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Creatinine: Typically normal (required baseline if considering verapamil and/or lithium)

medications: Oxygen 100% 12-15 L/min non-rebreather mask at onset for 15-20 minutes PRN attack
Sumatriptan 6 mg subcutaneous once at onset; may repeat after 1 hour if needed (max 12 mg/24 h)
Verapamil immediate-release 80 mg PO three times daily; titrate every 7-14 days to effect with ECG monitoring
Prednisone 60 mg PO daily for 5 days then taper by 10 mg every 2 days (bridge therapy while verapamil titrated)
non_pharmacological: Avoid alcohol during cluster periods (common trigger)
Maintain consistent sleep schedule; avoid sleep deprivation
Headache diary documenting timing, duration, triggers, and response to abortives
Chronic bronchitis counseling: smoking cessation and avoidance of airway irritants (also reduces headache trigger burden)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks for response and verapamil titration/ECG review; sooner (24-72 hours) if attacks not controlled or red flags develop

must_include: Diagnosis is cluster headache (a trigeminal autonomic cephalalgia), not sinus infection despite rhinorrhea
How it differs from trigeminal neuralgia (seconds-long shock-like triggered pain vs longer attacks with autonomic symptoms)
Expected course: attacks occur in clusters; may remit between cluster periods; familial tendency possible
Acute attack plan: high-flow oxygen and injectable triptan at onset
Preventive plan: verapamil as first-line prevention; steroids are short-term bridge only
When to seek urgent care: new neurologic deficits, atypical first/worst headache, persistent fever/neck stiffness, chest pain or severe shortness of breath after triptan
Review cardiovascular risk/contraindications to triptans (CAD, stroke/TIA, uncontrolled hypertension) and verapamil (bradycardia/heart block, hypotension)
Discuss chronic bronchitis considerations: oxygen use is generally safe; ensure appropriate delivery and fire safety; emphasize smoking cessation
Penicillin allergy noted; reassure that planned headache therapies are not penicillins
Avoid alcohol during active cluster periods
Sleep regularity and trigger avoidance
Use a headache diary and bring it to follow-up to guide titration and differential reassessment

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit headache phenotype and red flags to support cluster headache diagnosis and exclude secondary causes (timing, duration, laterality, severity, autonomic features, restlessness, triggers incl alcohol, circadian pattern, prior similar clusters, family history, neuro deficits, fever/neck stiffness, thunderclap/first-worst).

Assess comorbidities and risk factors relevant to therapy selection (cardiovascular history, uncontrolled HTN, stroke/TIA, arrhythmia/heart block, baseline bradycardia, pregnancy, smoking/chronic bronchitis/COPD, current meds including ergotamines/MAOIs/SSRIs/SNRIs, OTC/decongestants).
Critical safety check before prescribing; confirm reported penicillin allergy and any other drug allergies relevant to planned therapies.

Critical safety check before prescribing; screen for interactions (e.g., triptan with ergotamine within 24h, MAOIs for some triptans, serotonergic agents; verapamil with beta-blockers/digoxin and CYP3A4 inhibitors; lithium interactions if considered).

Clarify specific contraindications based on history (CAD/angina, prior MI, vasospasm/Prinzmetal, stroke/TIA, uncontrolled HTN for triptans; heart block, significant bradycardia, hypotension, HFrEF symptoms for verapamil).

Make and communicate diagnosis of familial cluster headache (TAC) based on recurrent unilateral severe attacks with autonomic symptoms and cluster periodicity; document differential considerations (sinusitis, trigeminal neuralgia, migraine, secondary headache) and why less likely.
Explain diagnosis milestone content, including why rhinorrhea can be autonomic and not infection; differentiate from trigeminal neuralgia; discuss expected course and familial tendency.

Initiate acute therapy plan: high-flow oxygen via non-rebreather at onset and a triptan appropriate for cluster (e.g., subcutaneous sumatriptan or intranasal zolmitriptan) if no contraindications; provide clear use instructions and limits.
If multiple prescriptions are issued (e.g., triptan plus verapamil/steroid bridge), re-confirm interactions across the full regimen and patient’s current meds.

Initiate preventive therapy: verapamil as first-line prevention with plan for titration and monitoring; consider short-term corticosteroid bridge only if frequent attacks while verapamil titrated and no contraindications.

Explain treatment milestone content: acute oxygen/triptan plan; preventive verapamil and steroid bridge rationale; explicit urgent care triggers including neurologic deficits, atypical first/worst headache, fever/neck stiffness, and chest pain/SOB after triptan.

Address concerns milestone content: review CV contraindications for triptans and verapamil conduction risks; chronic bronchitis considerations and oxygen/fire safety; smoking cessation; reassure penicillin allergy not relevant to planned therapies.
Lifestyle education milestone: avoid alcohol during cluster periods, sleep regularity/trigger avoidance, headache diary use and follow-up preparation.

Arrange timely follow-up to assess response, titrate verapamil, review diary, and reassess diagnosis; include monitoring plan for verapamil (e.g., ECG monitoring per local protocol) and medication safety.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

Critical contraindication check before ordering any invasive/high-risk diagnostics: ensure no contraindications to CT contrast (if planned), bronchoscopy/BAL (if needed), and evaluate Strongyloides risk prior to steroids (to prevent hyperinfection) with appropriate testing/treatment pathway when risk is present.

Arrange close follow-up (24–72h if outpatient; or post-discharge within 1 week), repeat oximetry and CBC/imaging as indicated, and ensure trigger evaluation plan is in place.

Recent viral upper respiratory infection symptoms (sore throat, myalgias) preceding illness can mimic influenza

College dormitory residence with close contacts increases exposure risk but may bias toward assuming meningococcal disease without full workup

correct_diagnosis.primary: Meningococcal infection (suspected invasive meningococcal disease: meningococcemia +/- meningitis)
required_evidence: Acute onset systemic illness compatible with meningococcal disease (e.g., fever, severe illness/toxicity, headache/neck stiffness or altered mental status, or petechial/purpuric rash)
CBC suggestive of bacterial infection (often leukocytosis with neutrophilia; may be leukopenia in severe sepsis)
Thrombocytopenia possible in severe disease/DIC
Low bicarbonate (CO2) possible due to metabolic acidosis in sepsis
Clinical urgency: treat immediately when suspected; do not delay for confirmatory testing

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Elevated (leukocytosis) or decreased if fulminant sepsis; typically abnormal
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Usually normal initially; may be low if hemorrhage/DIC or dilutional
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal to low; thrombocytopenia can occur with severe meningococcemia/DIC
expected_lab_results.Basic Metabolic Panel - Sodium: May be low (hyponatremia) due to SIADH/dehydration; can be normal
expected_lab_results.Basic Metabolic Panel - Potassium: Variable; may be high or low depending on acidosis/renal perfusion
expected_lab_results.Basic Metabolic Panel - Chloride: Often normal; may be elevated with non-anion gap acidosis from fluids

Norepinephrine IV infusion titrate to MAP >= 65 mmHg if hypotension persists after fluids
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO/IV every 6 hours as needed for fever/pain
non_pharmacological: Immediate droplet precautions
Urgent transfer/management in ED/ICU setting if unstable or concern for meningitis/sepsis
Obtain blood cultures promptly if feasible but do not delay antibiotics
Consider lumbar puncture when safe (after stabilization; and neuroimaging if indicated) without delaying antibiotics
Notify public health and arrange chemoprophylaxis for close contacts per local guidance
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Immediate reassessment within 15-30 minutes after initial therapy; ongoing inpatient monitoring with daily review and de-escalation when pathogen/susceptibility confirmed (typical total antibiotic duration 5-7 days for meningococcemia; 7 days for meningococcal meningitis).

must_include: This is suspected invasive meningococcal disease, a rapidly progressive bacterial infection that can cause sepsis and/or meningitis
It is a medical emergency; treatment is started immediately based on suspicion

Immediate IV antibiotics are required; ceftriaxone is first-line and is generally safe despite a remote penicillin-hives history

Explain why Sulfisoxazole is not appropriate for treating suspected invasive meningococcal disease

Importance of completing the full antibiotic course if discharged (rare; typically inpatient)
Vaccination counseling for meningococcal vaccines when appropriate after recovery and for household contacts per guidance
Return precautions: worsening headache, confusion, rash, breathing difficulty, fainting, or new bleeding/bruising

Chronic, gradually progressive course (months to years) rather than acute onset
Family history consistent with inherited ataxia OR onset pattern consistent with hereditary ataxia when family history is unknown
Exclusion of common reversible/toxic/metabolic causes with basic labs (no major electrolyte derangements; no hepatic/renal failure)

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis (progressive cerebellar disorder, hereditary possibility, no cure, red flags), explain treatment (goals, PT/OT/SLP core, medication risks, monitoring plan), address concerns (patient goals, falls, family implications), and lifestyle/home safety/swallowing/aspiration guidance.

Set defined follow-up timeframe to assess function, falls, side effects, vitals (if propranolol), sedation (if baclofen), and therapy progress; arrange neurology/genetic counseling referral if not already done.
Close encounter after ensuring safety netting, follow-up, and patient understanding.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Iron deficiency anemia
acceptable_alternatives: Anemia of chronic inflammation (anemia of chronic disease)
required_evidence: Low ferritin consistent with depleted iron stores
CBC pattern consistent with microcytic anemia (low MCV) with elevated RDW
No alternative explanation on available data (e.g., normal/high RBC count would suggest thalassemia trait rather than iron deficiency)

expected_lab_results.RBC count: Low to normal (often low/normal in iron deficiency; typically not high)
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Normal to high (reactive thrombocytosis can occur)

medications: Ferrous sulfate 325 mg (65 mg elemental iron) PO every other day
non_pharmacological: Assess and address source of iron loss (e.g., menstrual bleeding, GI blood loss); arrange appropriate evaluation based on age/sex and symptoms
Dietary counseling to increase iron-rich foods (heme and non-heme sources)
Administration counseling: take with water or vitamin C-containing beverage if tolerated; avoid taking with calcium/antacids/tea/coffee within 2 hours
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck CBC (and ferritin if available) in 4 weeks to assess response; continue iron for ~3 months after hemoglobin normalizes to replete stores

must_include: Labs suggest anemia due to low iron stores (low ferritin) with small red blood cells
Need to identify why iron is low (dietary deficiency vs blood loss) and not just replace iron
Oral iron dosing plan and expected timeline of improvement (energy first; blood counts improve over weeks)
Common side effects (GI upset, constipation, dark stools) and how to manage
Importance of continuing iron after hemoglobin normalizes to rebuild iron stores
Red flags requiring urgent care (chest pain, syncope, severe shortness of breath, black/tarry stools, significant rectal bleeding)
When to contact clinician for intolerance or lack of improvement
Iron-rich diet suggestions and pairing non-heme iron with vitamin C
Avoiding inhibitors of absorption around dosing (calcium, antacids, tea/coffee)

Agent provides diet counseling: iron-rich foods, pairing non-heme iron with vitamin C, and avoiding absorption inhibitors around dosing (calcium, antacids, tea/coffee).
